terms.SaaS: Cloud-based software delivery model where programs are licensed on subscription basis, hosted by developer, accessed via web interface. Generates predictable recurring revenue through monthly, quarterly or annual fees.
terms.Freemium_Model: Blends 'free' and 'premium' access — users start with no-cost plan delivering essential functionality. Converts free users to paid through feature gates, usage limits, limited storage, or limited support. Common conversion rates: 2-5% of free users.
terms.Subscription_Model: Foundation of most SaaS businesses generating predictable recurring revenue. Variants: monthly (MRR focus), annual (ARR focus, better cash flow), usage-based, seat-based.
terms.Marketplace: Digital platform facilitating transactions between multiple buyers and sellers. Aggregates many sellers under one roof. Revenue via commission (% of transaction), listing fees, subscriptions, or premium features. Examples: Amazon, Airbnb, Uber.
terms.Commission_Model: Revenue model where marketplace charges a percentage or fixed fee when customer pays supplier. Most popular online marketplace business model. Also called take rate.
terms.Usage_Based_Pricing: Customers pay based on actual consumption (API calls, data processed, seats used). Aligns cost with value. Common in infrastructure (AWS), data (Snowflake), and communications (Twilio).
terms.Tiered_Pricing: Multiple pricing tiers (e.g., Starter, Pro, Enterprise) with increasing features and price points. Allows market segmentation and upsell path.
terms.Open_Core: Core product open source, premium features proprietary. Drives adoption via free tier, monetizes through enterprise features. Examples: GitLab, Elasticsearch, MongoDB.
terms.API_First: Product built as API first, enabling developer adoption and integration. Revenue via API call volume, subscriptions. Examples: Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid.

terms.GDP: Total value of all goods and services produced within a country. Nominal GDP measures entire value; Real GDP adjusts for price changes. Increase indicates growing economy; decrease suggests slowdown. Formula: GDP = C + I + G + (X-M).
terms.CPI: Consumer Price Index — measures average change over time in prices paid by urban consumers for a basket of goods and services. Most widely used measure of inflation. Affects monetary policy, wage negotiations, and financial planning.
terms.PMI: Purchasing Managers' Index — monthly survey of senior executives about new orders, production, employment, inventory, supplier deliveries. PMI > 50 = expansion; PMI < 50 = contraction. Leading economic indicator.
terms.Inflation_Rate: Rate at which general level of prices rises, eroding purchasing power. Measured via CPI, PCE, WPI. Affects interest rates, bond yields, equity valuations, and real returns.
terms.Yield_Curve: Measures yield to maturity of bonds across maturities. Normal: upward sloping (higher yield for longer maturity). Inverted yield curve (short > long term yields) historically precedes recessions. Key signal for monetary policy expectations.
terms.Yield_Curve_Inversion: When short-term bond yields exceed long-term bond yields. Indicates market expects future rate cuts and slower growth. Historically one of most reliable recession predictors — inverted before every US recession since 1955.
terms.Fed_Funds_Rate: Interest rate at which US depository institutions lend reserve balances to each other overnight. Primary monetary policy tool of the Federal Reserve. Influences all other interest rates in the economy.
terms.Repo_Rate: Rate at which central bank (RBI in India, Fed in US) lends to commercial banks. Primary monetary policy instrument. Increase tightens liquidity and credit; decrease stimulates lending.
terms.VIX: CBOE Volatility Index — 'fear gauge' measuring market expectation of 30-day volatility in S&P 500. Calculated from options prices. VIX > 30 indicates high fear/uncertainty; VIX < 15 indicates complacency. Inverse correlation with equity markets.
terms.QE: Quantitative Easing — central bank purchases financial assets (bonds) to inject liquidity, lower long-term rates, and stimulate economy. Used when conventional monetary policy (rate cuts) insufficient. Expands central bank balance sheet.
terms.QT: Quantitative Tightening — reverse of QE. Central bank reduces balance sheet by allowing bonds to mature without reinvestment or actively selling holdings. Tightens financial conditions, raises long-term rates.

terms.Market_Risk: Losses due to changes in market conditions — stock prices, interest rates, exchange rates, commodity prices. For startups: risk that market timing is wrong or market doesn't develop as expected.
terms.Credit_Risk: Risk that counterparty fails to fulfill contractual obligations resulting in non-payment or delayed payment. For startups: customer default risk on receivables; for investors: portfolio company default.
terms.Operational_Risk: Losses from broken processes, systems, or human errors — technology failures, supply chain disruptions, fraud, regulatory non-compliance. Basel III defines it as risk of loss from inadequate or failed internal processes, people, systems, or external events.
terms.Liquidity_Risk: Risk of being unable to meet short-term financial obligations without taking a loss. For startups: running out of cash (high burn, slow fundraising). For funds: inability to exit investments.
terms.Regulatory_Risk: Risk of changes in laws, regulations, or their interpretation that adversely affect business. High for fintech, healthtech, crypto, edtech. Can materially change business model viability.
terms.Reputational_Risk: Risk of damage to brand, trust, and relationships from negative publicity, product failures, leadership misconduct, or customer complaints. Can be existential for consumer businesses.
terms.Cyber_Risk: Risk of financial loss, disruption, or reputational damage from cyberattacks — data breaches, ransomware, DDoS, phishing. Growing with digital transformation. Mitigation: SOC2, ISO 27001, penetration testing.
terms.Key_Man_Risk: Dependence on one or few critical individuals whose departure would significantly harm the business. High in early-stage startups. Mitigated by key man insurance, succession planning, knowledge documentation.
terms.Four_Risk_Responses: Avoidance (eliminate the risk's cause), Mitigation (reduce probability/impact), Transference (insurance, hedging), Acceptance (accept residual risk with contingency plan).

terms.India_Startup_Ecosystem_2025: India startup funding hit $11B in 2025 (TechCrunch). Over $12.1B raised in new VC/PE funds in 2025 (up 39% YoY). Deep-tech companies raised USD 1.06B across 137 rounds by July 2025 (double YoY).
terms.India_Fintech: Largest startup sector in India. $1.6B across 68 deals in 2024. Accounts for ~16% of new fund corpus in 2025. Driven by UPI, BNPL, neobanks, lending, wealthtech. Key players: Razorpay, PhonePe, Paytm, CRED, Groww, Zerodha.
terms.India_Healthtech: 28.03% rise in funding signals strong investor confidence. Key companies: TrueMeds ($85M Series C). Driven by digital health, telemedicine, diagnostics, health insurance.
terms.India_Logistics_Tech: 104% growth from H2 2024. Driven by electric mobility, climate-focused logistics, quick commerce. Quick commerce platforms like Zepto raised $450M.
terms.India_Edtech: Received $13.1B in total funding over last decade from national and global investors. Post-BYJU's crash, sector consolidating. Focus on vernacular learning, B2B EdTech, test prep.
terms.India_AgriTech: Government launched INR 750 Cr 'AgriSURE' fund for agritech and rural startups. Focus on farm-to-fork, precision farming, FPO digitization, input marketplaces.
terms.India_DeepTech: Fastest growing segment. USD 1.06B raised in H1 2025 alone. Focus on AI/ML, semiconductors, defense tech, space tech, quantum computing.
